title: Collision-induced fragmentation of underivatizedN-linked carbohydrates ionized by electrospray
abstract:
JOURNAL OF MASS SPECTROMETRY
J. Mass Spectrom. 35, 1178–1190 (2000)
Collision-induced fragmentation of underivatized
N -linked carbohydrates ionized by electrospray
David J. Harvey*
Glycobiology Institute, Department of Biochemistry, South Parks Road, Oxford OX1 3QU, UK
The electrospray mass spectra and collision-induced fragmentation of neutral N -linked glycans obtained from
glycoproteins were examined with a Q-TOF mass spectrometer. The glycans were ionized most effectively as
adducts of alkali metals, with lithium providing the most abundant signal and caesium the least. Singly charged
ions generally gave higher ion currents than doubly charged ions. Addition of formic acid could be used to
produce [M Y H]Y ions, but these ions were always accompanied by abundant cone-voltage fragments. The
energy required for collision-induced fragmentation was found to increase in a linear manner as a function of
mass with the [M Y Na]Y ions requiring about four times as much energy as the [M Y H]Y ions for complete
fragmentation of the molecular ions. Fragmentation of the [M Y H]Y ions gave predominantly B- and Y-type
glycosidic fragments whereas the [M Y Na]Y and [M Y Li]Y ions produced a number of additional fragments
including those derived from cross-ring cleavages. Little fragmentation was observed from the [M Y K]Y and
[M Y Rb]Y ions and the only fragment to be observed from the [M Y Cs]Y ion was CsY. The [M Y Na]Y and
[M Y Li]Y ions from all the N -linked glycans gave abundant fragments resulting from loss of the terminal
GlcNAc moiety and prominent, though weaker, ions as the result of 0,2A and 2,4A cross-ring cleavages of this
residue. Most other ions were the result of successive additional losses of residues from the non-reducing
terminus. This pattern was particularly prominent with glycans containing several non-reducing GlcNAc
residues where successive losses of 203 u were observed. Many of the ions in the low-mass range were products
of several different fragmentation routes but still provided structural information. Possibly of most diagnostic
importance was an ion formed by loss of 221 u (GlcNAc molecule) from an ion that had lost the 3-antenna
and the chitobiose core. This latter ion, although coincident in mass with some other ‘internal’ fragments, often
provided additional information on the composition of the antennae. Other ions deﬁning antenna composition
were weak cross-ring fragments produced from the core branching mannose residue. Glycans containing Gal-
GlcNAc residues showed successive losses of this moiety, particularly from the B-type fragments resulting from
loss of the reducing-terminal GlcNAc residue. The [M Y Na]Y and [M Y Li]Y ions from high-mannose and
hybrid glycans gave a series of ions of composition (Man)nNa/LiY where n = 1 to the total number of glycans in
the molecule, allowing these sugars to be distinguished from the more highly processed complex glycans. Other
ions in the spectra of the high-mannose glycans were diagnostic of chain branching but insufﬁcient information
was available to determine their mode of formation. Copyright 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
KEYWORDS: carbohydrates; electrospray; fragmentation; collision-induced dissociation; glycosidic cleavage
INTRODUCTION
Extensive studies of the fragmentation of complex gly-
cans have been performed over the last 20 years,1–11 but,
even today, it is difﬁcult to extract a full structure from a
single spectrum. Unlike the case with linear biopolymers,
such as peptides and oligonucleotides, where monomer
type and sequence are the only parameters to be deter-
mined, structural analysis of glycans requires the addi-
tional determination of branching, linkage position and
monomer anomericity. Furthermore, many of the con-
stituent monosaccharides, such as mannose, galactose and
glucose, are isobaric and can usually only be identiﬁed by
* Correspondence to: D. J. Harvey, Glycobiology Institute, Depart-
ment of Biochemistry, South Parks Road, Oxford OX1 3QU, UK.
E-mail: dh@glycob.ox.ac.uk
Contract/grant sponsor: Biotechnology and Biological Sciences
Research Council.
gas chromatography/mass spectrometry (GC/MS) or their
susceptibility to exoglycosidases. Carbohydrates ionized
by different ionization techniques tend to show similar
fragmentation pathways, although the extent to which a
given pathway is observed will depend on factors such
as the energy imparted to the ion, the nature of the
adduct inducing ionization and the lifetime of the ion
before detection. Thus, high-energy collision-induced dis-
sociation (CID) spectra obtained with magnetic sector
instruments12,13 differ considerably from, say, post-source
decay (PSD) spectra recorded with time-of-ﬂight (TOF)
instruments where energies are lower and ion lifetimes
(with delayed-extraction ion sources) longer. The exten-
sive fragmentation obtained with magnetic sector instru-
ments is usually at the expense of sensitivity and PSD
fragmentation is often of relatively low resolution. With
the advent of the newer mass spectrometric instrumenta-
tion such as Q-TOF-type mass spectrometers, it is pos-
sible to obtain complex fragmentation spectra carrying
much structural information at both high sensitivity and
Copyright 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Received 18 February 2000
Accepted 20 July 2000
 N-LINKED CARBOHYDRATE FRAGMENTATION
1179
at relatively high resolution. However, the interpretation
of such spectra is complex and, as yet, poorly understood.
Carbohydrates show basically three types of fragmenta-
tion of which two, glycosidic cleavages involving a single
bond cleavage between adjacent sugar rings and cross-
ring fragmentation requiring the cleavage of two bonds,
provide structural information. The third type, seen from
alkali metal adducts, is loss of the metal,14 but the result-
ing metal ion carries no structural information. Glycosidic
cleavages, which generally predominate and are thought
to be charged-induced,14 give information on sequence
and branching but little on linkage. Cross-ring cleavages,
on the other hand, which appear to be charge-remote
processes, are much more useful for linkage determi-
nation. The nomenclature used by most investigators to
describe fragmentation is that introduced by Domon and
Costello.15 Ions containing the reducing terminus are des-
ignated X (cross-ring), Y (1-O glycosidic link) or Z,
whereas those from the non-reducing end of the molecule
are designated A (cross-ring), B and C. Subscript numerals
denote the position of cleavage and superscript numerals
attached to the cross-ring fragments show the bonds that
are broken.
There have been many studies of the fragmentation
of complex glycans ionized by fast atom bombard-
ment (FAB)12,16 and some investigators still use this
mode of operation.17 Most investigators using this tech-
nique have studied carbohydrates as their permethyl
derivatives, partly because derivatization is essential to
cause the molecules to ‘ﬂy’ under FAB ionization condi-
tions but, more importantly, because, following glycosidic
Y-type fragmentation, a hydroxy rather than a methoxy
group is left at the site of attachment of the expelled
group, thus yielding linkage information.8 With the advent
of matrix-assisted laser desorption ionization (MALDI)
mass spectrometry, it has been found that permethy-
lation is unnecessary in order to obtain a signal,18–20
although permethylation usually results in improved sensi-
tivity with most ionization techniques.21,22 Permethylation
can, however, be problematic for small sample quanti-
ties owing to the clean-up that is necessary in order to
obtain a spectrum. Reducing-terminal derivatives, such
as those containing tertiary amines,23,24 basic peptides,25
hydrophobic,26,27 cationic,28–31 anionic32–34 or other29,35
groups have also been used to improve detection sensitiv-
ity for FAB, MALDI or electrospray ionization, but most
appear to have little fragmentation-directing abilities,36,37
although fragments from the derivative itself have been
reported in limited cases.24 We, and others have there-
fore examined the fragmentation of underivatized glycans
with the hope of extracting similar information to that
obtainable from the spectra of their permethylated ana-
logues. Although spectra are generally more difﬁcult to
interpret, fragment ion yields are often higher on account
of the ready migration of hydroxylic hydrogen atoms dur-
ing glycosidic cleavage.
Following MALDI ionization, fragment ions can be
observed as in-source fragments38,39 provided that their
fragmentation rate constants are sufﬁciently high and
source residence times are sufﬁcient.39 More commonly,
however, they are studied as post-source decay (PSD)
fragments. In-source fragments are particularly abundant
when observed with a magnetic sector instrument38 but
can only be seen in the upper mass range because of
interference by matrix ions in the low-mass region. PSD
ions from sugars also tend to be concentrated in the upper
mass region,38,40 resolution is often moderate and, with
most TOF instruments, spectra have to be recorded in
segments in order to preserve resolution over the entire
mass range. High-energy CID spectra13 have so far given
the most informative fragmentation patterns from glycans
ionized by MALDI with ions spread over the entire mass
range. Unfortunately, ion yields are low and, because the
rate of spectral acquisition is limited to the laser repetition
rate, this is slow with up to 30 min being necessary to
obtain a spectrum.
Electrospray ionization has, so far, made less of an
impact on glycan analysis although it enables fragmen-
tation spectra to be obtained much more rapidly on
account of the non-pulsed nature of the ion beam. Nano-
electrospray appears particularly attractive on account of
its high signal-to-noise ratio.41 Some of the most promis-
ing applications involve the use of ion trap41–44 or Fourier
transform ion cyclotron resonance (FTICR) instruments45
by utilizing their ability to perform successive fragmenta-
tion reactions at high yield. Again, permethylated sugars
have been preferred. With this technique it is possi-
ble to obtain small fragments from speciﬁc areas of
the molecules and to fragment these to obtain detailed
structural information, including linkage.
With the advent of the electrospray Q-TOF instru-
ment, it is now possible to obtain fragmentation under
controlled collision conditions from continuous beams
offering the means to obtain fragmentation spectra in
a short time with high mass accuracy. The Q-TOF
instrument has been exploited by several groups who
have studied the fragmentation behaviour of doubly and
triply charged ions from carbohydrates derivatized with
2-aminoacridone,46–49 2-aminopyridine50 or 2-(diethyl-
amino)ethyl-4-aminobenzoate.50 We, however, have found
that equally strong or stronger signals can be obtained
from the singly charged alkali metal-adducted ions of
underivatized glycans and that the fragmentation spectra
offer several advantages over those of the doubly charged
ions. Previous work using MALDI ionization51 has shown
that the lighter alkali metals, lithium and sodium, produce
the strongest fragmentation spectra whereas rubidium and
caesium adducts are extremely difﬁcult to fragment. The
type of fragmentation is also dependent on the nature
of the molecular ions with [M C H]C ions, generated by
MALDI, fragmenting almost exclusively by glycosidic
cleavage. Ions containing metal adducts, on the other
hand, fragment to give both glycosidic and cross-ring
products.52 This paper examines the use of the Q-TOF
instrument for carbohydrate analysis with the emphasis on
the fragmentation of singly charged ions produced from
N-linked glycans released from glycoproteins.
EXPERIMENTAL
Materials
Maltoheptaose was obtained from Sigma Chemical (Poole,
Dorset, UK). N-Linked glycans were obtained from
Oxford GlycoSciences (Abingdon, Oxfordshire, UK) or
were released from glycoproteins (ribonuclease-B and
Copyright 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
J. Mass Spectrom. 35, 1178–1190 (2000)

Maltoheptaose was obtained from Sigma Chemical (Poole,
Dorset, UK). N-Linked glycans were obtained from
Oxford GlycoSciences (Abingdon, Oxfordshire, UK) or
were released from glycoproteins (ribonuclease-B and
Copyright 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
chicken ovalbumin obtained from Sigma Chemical) with
hydrazine.53 The chicken ovalbumin sample was found to
contain other glycoproteins including chicken riboﬂavin
Dihydroxybenzoic acid (DHB) was obtained from Aldrich
Electrospray mass spectrometry
Electrospray mass spectrometry (MS) and MS/MS were
performed with a Micromass Q-TOF mass spectrometer
(Micromass (UK), Wythenshawe, Manchester, UK). The
carbohydrates, dissolved in methanol–water (1 : 1, v/v)
at a concentration of about 10 pmol µl1, were infused
using the nanoﬂow probe at between 50 and 200 nl min1.
Salts and other additives were added to this solvent as
described below. The needle voltage was 3000 V and the
ion source was maintained at 80 °C. Argon was used as
the collision gas at a pressure of 20 psi and the colli-
sion energy and cone voltage were adjusted as described
below. The resolution of MS1 was set to unit mass. The
spectral acquisition rate was 48 spectra min1 (including
inter-scan delay) and the signal was accumulated until a
satisfactory signal-to-noise ratio had been obtained.
Effect of collision energy on fragmentation. A solution of dex-
tran oligomers was infused at 200 nl min1 in 1 : 1 (v/v)
water–methanol as above with the cone voltage set to
maximize the abundance of either the [M C H]C or [M C
Na]C ion. Molecular ions were selected in turn and the
voltage on the collision cell was raised until the molecular
ion just disappeared from the CID spectrum. The voltage
was recorded and plotted against the number of glucose
residues in the dextran oligomer being examined.
Positive ion MALDI mass spectra were recorded with a
Micromass TofSpec 2E mass spectrometer in reﬂectron
mode. The delay for the time-lag-focusing ion source was
500 ns, the pulse voltage was 3.4 kV and the acceler-
ation voltage was 20 kV. Spectra from about 30 laser
shots were averaged to produce each spectrum and pro-
cessing was performed with a MassLynx data system. For
sample preparation, the glycan solution (0.5 µl containing
10 pmol of glycan) was applied to the stainless-steel tar-
get along with the matrix (0.5 µl of a saturated solution of
DHB in acetonitrile) and allowed to dry. The sample was
then recrystallized from the minimum volume of ethanol.
Effect of cation. Formic acid (0.1–1.0%) or salts contain-
ing a variety of cations (¾50 nmol µl1), were added to a
solution of maltoheptaose (I, 10 pmol), or N-linked gly-
cans released from ribonuclease B, in water–methanol
(1 : 1, v/v) and infused at 0.2 µl min1 in order to pro-
duce [M C H]C, [M C metal]C or various doubly charged
ions. With formic acid (1.0%) as the additive to the high-
mannose glycan ▷GlcNAc◁2▷Man◁5 (II), the most abun-
dant singly charged molecular ion was [M C H]C, the
abundance of which maximized at a cone voltage of about
50 V (Fig. 1). However, in-source fragments of this ion
(m/z 1014 and 528) were much more abundant and dom-
inated the spectrum at cone voltages above 60 V. The
[M C Na]C ion increased in abundance up to a cone volt-
age of 140 V, at which point it was about one third as
abundant as the maximum abundance of the [M C H]C
ion. Its fragment ions (m/z 1036, 671 and 509, shown in
Fig. 1) started to appear at about 100 V but only reached
about half the abundance of the [M C Na]C ion at the
maximum energy attainable (200 V). The only doubly
charged ion that was observed was [M C H C K]2C. Its
relative abundance paralleled that of the [M C H]C ion.
Addition of a small amount (¾50 nmol µl1) of sodium
acetate or iodide produced only [M C Na]C, [M C 2Na]2C
and some weak fragment ions. Although the absolute
abundance of the [M C 2Na]2C ions was relatively inde-
pendent of the cone voltage, that of the [M C Na]C ion
increased dramatically to about three times the abundance
of the [M C 2Na]2C ion at higher voltages with a maxi-
mum abundance at about 170 V. Cone voltage fragments
were present, but fragmentation was much less than from
the [M C H]C ions (Fig. 2). Addition of larger amounts of
sodium acetate caused replacement of some of the labile
hydrogen atoms by sodium and gave a series of ions
spaced by 22 u at higher masses from all of the major
fragment ions. At low cone voltages (range 5–80 V)
under these conditions of high sodium concentration, the
[M C 2Na]2C ion was accompanied by a series of doubly
Figure 1. Plot of selected ion abundances for ▷GlcNAc◁2▷Man◁5
(II) as a function of cone voltage when sprayed from 1 : 1 (v/v)
methanol water containing 1.0% formic acid. The ions at m/z
1036 and 1014 are the B4 fragment ions from the [M C Na]C and
[M C H]C ions, respectively. Ions at m/z 617 and 509 are further
fragments of the [M C Na]C ion and m/z 528 is from the [M C H]C
ion. The ion at m/z 637 is a doubly charged ion ▷[M C H C K]2C◁.
Copyright 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
N-LINKED CARBOHYDRATE FRAGMENTATION
Figure 2. Positive ion electrospray mass spectrum of N-linked glycans from ribonuclease B when sprayed from 1 : 1 (v/v)
methanol water containing 50 nmol µl1 of sodium acetate and recorded at a cone voltage of 170 V.°
charged ions containing sodium acetate. Replacement of
the hydrogen by sodium in acidic groups such as sialic
acids and sulphate to give the sodium salts is common
at low levels of sodium but replacement of hydroxylic
hydrogen atoms was only observed under conditions of
These results demonstrate that the positive ion electro-
spray mass spectrum of carbohydrates can be very vari-
able, depending on the concentration of various cations
in the solvent and on the nozzle–skimmer (cone) volt-
age. Under some conditions, abundant fragments of [M C
H]C ions were observed even though the only signiﬁ-
cant molecular ion in the spectrum was [M C Na]C. Thus,
both solvent composition and ion source conditions should
be carefully controlled in order to obtain high sensitivity
(single molecular ion) and unambiguous in-source frag-
The ability of other cations to ionize glycans was stud-
ied by adding equimolar amounts of lithium, sodium,
potassium, rubidium and caesium iodide ▷50 nmol µl1◁
to a solution of ▷GlcNAc◁2▷Man◁5 from ribonuclease B
in methanol–water (1 : 1, v/v). The strongest signals were
obtained with lithium (Fig. 3) with a progressive fall
as the atomic mass of the adduct increased. The situ-
ation following ionization by MALDI from DHB was
more erratic with the strongest signal being recorded with
sodium (Fig. 3). This result from MALDI is similar to that
reported by others,55 but may be slightly biased towards
sodium on account of residual sodium in the matrix and
the fact that the lithium adducts may have undergone more
Collision-induced fragmentation
The remaining discussion of fragmentation refers to that
occurring within the collision cell of the Q-TOF instru-
ment and not that observed in the ion source.
Figure 3. Comparison of the electrospray ionization and MALDI
signal strengths of the singly charged ions obtained from
▷GlcNAc◁2▷Man◁5 (II) when ionized with various cations.
Effect of collision energy. The increase in MS/MS fragmen-
tation with collision energy was monitored under CID
conditions with the isolated [M C H]C ion (m/z 1153.3)
from maltoheptaose as shown in Fig. 4. Fragmentation
occurred by glycosidic cleavage with the most abun-
dant fragment ion progressively shifting to lower mass
with increase in energy. Ions containing one to six glu-
cose residues appeared at m/z 161.1, 325.1, 487.2, 649.2,
811.3 and 973.3, respectively. At 60 V, the major frag-
ment contained one hexose residue (m/z 163.1) and at
higher energies this ion fragmented by successive losses
of water and a cross-ring fragmentation to give m/z 85.0
[C4H4O2 C H]C as the most abundant ion at energies
above 90 V. The singly charged metal adduct ions, on
the other hand, were very much more stable and needed
over 100 V for maximum fragmentation.

Effect of molecular mass. The energy required to fragment
the molecules increased with molecular mass as shown
in Fig. 5 for the [M C H]C and [M C Na]C ions from
dextrans containing different numbers of hexose residues.
The N-linked glycans required comparable amounts of
collision energy depending on the number of residues.
More energy was needed to fragment the [M C Na]C ions.
This effect presented problems for spectral reproducibil-
ity when a mixture of glycans was being examined as
optimum conditions were different for each compound. A
compromise was to record each spectrum over a range
of collision energies (usually about š10 V from the opti-
mum). This technique produced all possible fragment ions
in reasonable abundance but perturbed the abundance rela-
tionship between ions at opposite ends of the spectrum.

Effect of the adduct. As found by other investigators,51,56 the
energy needed to fragment the molecular ions increased
with the adduct in the order H < Li < Na < K. It was
difﬁcult to fragment ions from the glycans ionised with
rubidium or caesium (Fig. 6). In the case of caesium,
only the CsC fragment ion was observed. Formation of
the other fragment ions is discussed below. As noted
by other investigators,52,57,58 more cross-ring fragments
were produced from the [M C Li]C and [M C Na]C ions
than from the protonated ions. Lithium adducts gave
spectra similar to those of the sodium adducts, although
some other reports52 suggest that they should enhance

35 V) gave three major primary losses: water, a man-
nose residue (162 u, Y-cleavage) and a GlcNAc molecule
(221 u) as the result of a B-type cleavage from the reduc-
ing terminal (Fig. 7). The B and Y ions fragmented fur-
ther by successive losses of all mannose residues. The
spectrum thus differs from that of the [M C H C Na]2C
ion from the 2-AMAC derivative reported by Charlwood
et al.46 in that the latter spectrum did not contain the
prominent B and [B  ▷Man◁n] ion series. The only other
signiﬁcant ions in the spectrum of the [M C H]C ion
of the underivatized glycan were those corresponding to
[▷Man◁n C H]C where n D 1–4. This pattern of fragmen-
tation was repeated with the [M C H]C ions of other N-
linked glycans which essentially gave only B and Y ions
together with prominent internal fragments.
Fragmentation of lithium and sodium adducts. The MS/MS
fragmentation of the singly charged lithium and sodium
adducts of the glycans was more complicated and infor-
mative than that of the [M C H]C ions, as previously
observed30,59 for other glycans, because of the produc-
tion of several cross-ring and glycosidic cleavage ions.
The increased fragmentation is thought to be the result of
the ability of the sodium atom to coordinate oxygen atoms
in various positions of the molecule.
▷GlcNAc◁2▷Man◁3. All of the known N-linked glycans
contain the common trimannosyl-chitobiose core struc-
ture ▷GlcNAc◁2▷Man◁3 (IV). Its tandem mass spectrum
(sodium adduct) is shown in Fig. 8 with the proposed
cleavages outlined in the inset. The major cleavages were
glycosidic and involved losses of one ▷B3◁and two ▷B2◁
GlcNAc residues. Further losses of mannose residues
gave the internal cleavage ions shown in Fig. 8. Y1 and
Y2 fragments were prominent but the Y3 fragment was
weak. C-type fragments accompanied the B fragments and
most internal ions. Cross-ring fragments were relatively
abundant. 0,2A4 (m/z 832.2), 2,4A4 (m/z 772.2), 0,2A3 (m/z
629.2) and 2,4A3 (m/z 583.2) ions deﬁned the 1 ! 4 link-
ages of the GlcNAc residues and the 0,4A2 (m/z 245.0),
0,3A2 (m/z 275.0) and 3,5A2 (m/z 259.0) ions deﬁned the
branching around the core mannose residue. These cross-
ring cleavage ions were often weaker in the spectra of
larger glycans but it should be possible to produce this
▷GlcNAc◁2▷Man◁3 moiety from larger glycans by remov-
ing the antennae in an MSn-type experiment using an ion
trap as has been reported for permethylated glycans.43
High-mannose glycans. The tandem mass spectra
of the monosodium adducts of ▷GlcNAc◁2▷Man◁5 (II),
▷GlcNAc◁2▷Man◁6 (III) and ▷GlcNAc◁2▷Man◁9 (V) are
shown in Fig. 9 (collision cell voltage 80 V). The
spectra were similar to those recorded earlier using high
(800 eV) collision energy on a tandem sector/oa-TOF
instrument13 with the major exception that the 1,5X ions,
prominent at high energy, were absent. The 0,2A and
2,4A cross-ring ions were particularly prominent from
the reducing-terminal GlcNAc moiety (m/z 1156.3 and

and 599.2, respectively). These ions corresponded to

of these ions were observed to lose additional mannose
residues to give fragments in the m/z 400–500 region from
▷GlcNAc◁2▷Man◁5 and at higher masses in the spectra of
the larger high-mannose glycans.
Most of the major ions in the spectra of the high-
mannose glycans were formed by glycosidic cleavages.
The most abundant of these were the ions formed by loss
of one or both GlcNAc residues and the related internal
fragments that had lost additional mannose residues. The
relatively high abundance of the internal fragment at m/z
671 from ▷GlcNAc◁2▷Man◁5, arising from the B4 ion and
containing four mannose residues, was accounted for by
loss of the mannose residue from the 3-antenna. This
ion, termed ion D, was useful in deﬁning the branching
pattern and has been noted before13,54 as being one of the
most useful ions in both high-energy and PSD spectra of
these glycans for deﬁning chain branching. Its abundance
was also elevated above that of the other ions of the
▷Man◁xNaC series (m/z 185.0, 347.1, 509.1, 671.2, 833.2,
995.3, 1157.4, 1319.4 and 1481.5) in the spectrum of
▷GlcNAc◁2▷Man◁6 (III) [Fig. 9(b)], consistent with the
known60 presence of the sixth mannose residue attached to
the 3-antenna. It also appeared with the appropriate m/z
ratios in the spectra of the other high-mannose glycans
The relative abundances of internal fragments aris-
ing from the ion that had lost the reducing-terminal
GlcNAc residue [B4 ion from ▷GlcNAc◁2▷Man◁5 and
▷GlcNAc◁2▷Man◁6 and B5 ion from the other high-
mannose glycans] appeared to reﬂect the branching pat-

the internal fragment ions appeared to be formed by

and many contained losses of either or both GlcNAc
residues from the non-reducing terminus. The spectrum
also contained prominent ions due to the loss of sin-

Biantennary complex glycans. Fragmentation of the
core region of the [M C Na]C ion from the biantennary
glycan (VII) [Fig. 11(a)] followed the same general pat-
tern as that from ▷GlcNAc◁2▷Man◁5 with the 0,2A6, 2,4A6,
B5 and B4 ions being prominent. Cleavage of the antennae
adjacent to the GlcNAc residues produced abundant B2
and Y4 ions. In the spectrum of the analogue substi-
tuted with fucose at the six position of the core Glc-
NAc residue (data not shown), the presence of fucose
was revealed by the loss of 367 u (GlcNAc-Fuc in a
B-cleavage) rather than 221 u (GlcNAc, B5 cleavage)
to give the ion at m/z 1442.3 as in Fig. 11(a). Several
prominent internal fragments were present, particularly
involving B5 and B4 cleavages. The B4/Y3ˇ ion (ion D)
at m/z 712.2 (loss of the chitobiose core and 3-antenna)
was very abundant and conﬁrmed the composition of the
6-antenna. Previously, the relative abundance of this ion
in MALDI spectra recorded with a magnetic sector instru-
ment was successfully used to deﬁne the location of the
galactose residue in monogalactosylated biantennary gly-
cans from immunoglobulin G.16 In the spectra of other
Copyright 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
N-linked glycans, ion D shifted in mass as a function
of the composition of the 6-antenna. The weak 0,4A4,
3,5A4 and 0,3A4 cross-ring fragments at the core mannose
residue (m/z 610.2, 624.2 and 640.2, respectively) were
the most diagnostic of antenna composition. The weak-
ness of these cross-ring fragments is thought to be due to
competition with the very favourable cleavages adjacent
to the GlcNAc residues in the antennae. The 0,4A4 ion
was, unfortunately, also coincident in mass with potential
1,3A4 and 2,4A4 ions, thus limiting its diagnostic poten-
tial. The formation of the major ions is rationalized in
Large N-linked glycans from egg white glycoproteins.
These compounds are multi-antennary with many of their
non-reducing termini terminating in GlcNAc residues (e.g.
VIII). The absence of the ions at m/z 671.2 ▷Man◁4
and 833.2 ▷Man◁5 in their tandem mass spectra veriﬁed
the absence of a hybrid structures. The spectra were
essentially similar to those of the compounds derivatized
with 2-aminobenzamide at the reducing terminus and
published earlier.36 Cleavage adjacent to the GlcNAc
residues gave the dominant ions in their spectra. The
most abundant of these ion series originated from the
B4 ion (m/z 1930.7, loss of 221 u) and proceeded by
progressive losses of GlcNAc residues (203 u) to give
the ions at m/z 1727.6, 1524.5, 1321.3, 1118.3, 915.3
and 712.4, thus revealing the total number of antennae
and bisecting GlcNAc residues. Ion D (m/z 1159.4) was
weak but was observed to eliminate GlcNAc as 221 u to
give the ion at m/z 938.4, consistent with the presence
of bisecting GlcNAc residues. Cross-ring 0,2A5 and 2,4A5
ions and internal fragments formed by successive losses
of 203 u from these ions were prominent but the cross-
ring fragments originating from the core mannose residue
were too insigniﬁcant to be diagnostically useful. These
ions, however, appear to be more prominent in PSD
spectra.62 Because of the presence of many ions produced
by multiple losses of GlcNAc from various positions,
it was not possible to determine the branching pattern
unambiguously from these spectra.
The tandem mass spectrum of the monogalactosylated
derivative ▷Gal◁1▷GlcNAc◁8▷Man◁3 (IX) of this glycan
is shown in Fig. 12. The major isomer contained the
galactose substituted on the 6-antenna.63 This compound
produced a similar spectrum to that of the parent com-
pound but with the addition of a further series of ions
formed by successive losses of GlcNAc from the ion
formed by loss of a Gal-GlcNAc residue (m/z 1948.7,
Y4 ion, Fig. 12). This feature revealed the presence of
a non-reducing hexose residue and this was reﬂected by
the absence of a ▷Hex◁4 ion at m/z 671.2 which would
have been present if the glycan had a hybrid-type struc-
ture. However, the spectrum contained too many internal
fragments for the linkage position of the galactose residue
to be determined. Nevertheless, an ion at m/z 894.3 was
found in the spectra of all the glycans from chicken ovo-
mucoid/ovalbumin which contained a galactose residue
and three GlcNAc residues in the 6-antenna and not in the
spectra of the other compounds. It would appear that this
ion might be diagnostic for this structural feature. Its even
mass was consistent with the presence of three GlcNAc
residues (the ion contained sodium) leaving a mass differ-
ence of 262 u. This difference could be accommodated by
two hexose residues less 62 u which could be accounted
for by C2H6O2 (possibly HOCH2CH2OH). However, the
exact composition and mechanism of formation of this ion

voltage of 40 V, accompanied by a series of fragment ions

corresponding to successive losses of hexose residues and
to cross-ring cleavages leading to losses of 60, 78 and
120 u from each of these ions. The tandem mass spectrum,
which required a collision cell voltage of only 22 eV,
showed the same fragment ions. The spectra obtained from
the larger N-linked glycans were generally weak and not
as diagnostically useful as the positive ion spectra.
Comparison of the methods of ionization and fragmen-
tation of these glycans showed that the best results, in
terms of both signal strength and diagnostic utility of the
spectra, were obtained from the singly charged positive
ions formed from either lithium or sodium adduction. For-
mation of the [M C H]C ions was always accompanied
by [M C Na]C ions and abundant in-source fragments,
mainly arising from the [M C H]C ion. The resulting CID
spectra of the [M C H]C ions were less informative than
those of the [M C Na]C ions. The CID spectra of the
[M C Na]C ions contained many diagnostic fragments.
They were dominated by abundant, mainly B- and Y-type
glycosidic fragments, many of which were internal cleav-
age ions formed by several routes. The most abundant of
the glycosidic cleavage ions were produced by cleavages
adjacent to GlcNAc residues. These cleavages resulted in
abundant losses of the reducing-terminal GlcNAc residue,
with its attached fucose, if present, and losses of GlcNAc
or Gal-GlcNAc from glycans whose antennae terminated
in these groups. Thus, the primary glycosidic cleavage
ions provided information on the sequence of constituent
monosaccharides in these N-linked glycans and some
information on chain branching. They also allowed the
type of glycan, high-mannose, hybrid or complex to be
distinguished and provided information of fucose substi-
tution, particularly on the core GlcNAc residues.
Cross-ring fragments, other than those from the reduc-
ing-terminal GlcNAc residues, were generally less abun-
dant than the glycosidic cleavage ions and tended to
become less signiﬁcant as the number of GlcNAc residues
in the antennae increased. Ions of composition ▷Man◁n dif-
ferentiated high-mannose and hybrid glycans from com-
plex sugars and several ions, both cross-ring and gly-
cosidic, enabled the composition of the antennae to be
determined. An ion formed by loss of 221 u (GlcNAc
molecule) from the internal fragment ion that had lost
the 3-antenna and chitobiose core was the most reliable
indicator of the presence of a bisecting GlcNAc residue.
